cards.title: Claude Fable 5 stays in Max subscriptions

panels.head: Anthropic keeps Fable 5 in paid plans — competitive pressure wins
panels.sub: Starting July 20, Fable 5 is permanently included in Max and Team Premium subscriptions at 50% of usage limits, reversing the earlier plan to pull it behind API-only pricing.

panels.head: GPT-5.6 Sol made the original plan untenable
panels.sub: Anthropic had intended to reserve Fable 5 for API customers — but competing flagship models staying in rival subscriptions made a $100–$200/month plan without Anthropic's best model a hard sell.

panels.head: Tier breakdown matters: $20/month is still excluded
panels.sub: Max plans ($100 and $200/month) get Fable 5 at 50% limits. Pro and Team Standard users keep access via usage credits and receive a one-time $100 credit. The base $20/month Pro plan does not include Fable 5.

panels.head: Compute capacity was the original constraint — it still is
panels.sub: Anthropic's initial removal plan was driven by GPU scarcity. Keeping Fable 5 in subscriptions may require pulling back training compute to free capacity for inference — a real operational tradeoff to watch.

panels.head: If you're budgeting AI tooling, the tier gap just got wider
panels.sub: For teams using Fable 5 in production workflows, the 50%-of-limits cap on Max plans is the constraint to stress-test. If your workload hits that ceiling, API pricing — not subscription — may still be the right route.

cards.title: AI health confidant backed by real social data

panels.head: Health anxiety lives in a gap nobody owns
panels.sub: Millions of people Google symptoms, lurk on health subreddits, or say nothing at all — because asking a friend feels embarrassing and booking a doctor feels premature. That silent middle ground is the product opportunity.

panels.head: Anxious adults who self-censor before self-advocating
panels.sub: People who are uninsured, underinsured, or navigating a new diagnosis are most likely to delay care out of shame or confusion. They're already talking to AI chatbots — they just have no private, structured place to do it.

panels.head: Pick your confidant; get crowd-sourced context privately
panels.sub: The user describes a persona — 'sympathetic GP', 'older sibling who's a nurse' — and an AI adopts that voice. Behind the scenes, real social posts (Reddit, health forums via API) are retrieved to ground responses in what actual patients have experienced, without the user ever posting publicly.

panels.head: ChatGPT is generic; Reddit requires exposure
panels.sub: General LLMs lack the social-data grounding that makes advice feel relatable. Public forums require posting, which many people refuse. Telehealth bots are clinical and insurance-gated. None combine persona customisation, social listening, and full privacy.

panels.head: Hypothesis, not a product — test the shame signal first
panels.sub: Search r/HealthAnxiety and r/AskDocs for posts that say 'I didn't want to ask anyone but…' — count how many appear in the last 90 days and what condition categories dominate. That frequency and topic map is your early ICP and MVP scope, before writing a line of code.

cards.title: Space Force triples NSSL launch contract ceiling

panels.head: The U.S. national security launch budget just got 3× bigger
panels.sub: The ceiling on the National Security Space Launch Phase 3 Lane 1 contract jumped from $5.6 billion to $17 billion — a $11.4B expansion in a single amendment.

panels.head: Lane 1 is the competitive, high-volume tier of NSSL
panels.sub: NSSL Phase 3 splits launch work into two lanes: Lane 1 for competed, recurring missions and Lane 2 for sole-source sensitive payloads. ULA and SpaceX currently hold Lane 1 awards.

panels.head: Demand is outpacing what the original ceiling anticipated
panels.sub: Space Force raised the ceiling rather than re-compete the contract, signalling that classified and dual-use launch manifest growth is accelerating faster than planners projected circa 2022.

panels.head: More headroom means more national-security payloads reaching orbit
panels.sub: A higher ceiling unlocks additional task orders without a new procurement cycle, compressing timelines for constellation replenishment, ISR upgrades, and resilient comms — all areas where edge compute and onboard AI are increasingly embedded.

panels.head: $17B ceiling signals a sustained, not cyclical, surge
panels.sub: For launch providers and payload integrators alike, this is a policy signal: the U.S. government expects its national-security manifest to keep growing, not plateau — which supports multi-year investment in launch rate capacity.

panels.head: Moonshot AI ships the largest open-weight model yet
panels.sub: Kimi K3 has 2.8 trillion parameters — more than double DeepSeek's 1.6T V4 Pro — with weights dropping by July 27, 2026.

panels.head: Beats GPT-5.5 and Opus 4.8, trails Claude Fable 5
panels.sub: On Artificial Analysis's long-horizon eval, K3 scores Elo 1547, up 732 points from K2.6. It's also top of Arena.ai's Frontend Code leaderboard, above Fable 5.

panels.head: $3 in, $15 out — Chinese lab prices like a Western frontier model
panels.sub: That's roughly half the cost of Opus 4.8 ($1.80/task vs $0.94) but a 15× increase over Kimi K2.6's output price. K3 also uses 21% fewer output tokens than its predecessor, softening the bill.

panels.head: The pelican test taught something: it's now broken
panels.cmd: llm -m openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k3 'Generate an SVG of a pelican riding a bicycle'
panels.sub: For 21 months, generating a pelican-on-bicycle SVG correlated well with model quality. It no longer does — GLM-5.2 now beats Fable 5 on it. The test never touched agentic tool-calling, which is what actually differentiates today's frontier models.

panels.head: Benchmark your evals, not just your models
panels.sub: A proxy metric that worked can quietly stop working. For agentic or multi-step CV pipelines, validate that your internal evals still correlate with real task success — don't let them become pelicans.

cards.title: Pentagon pays to onshore satellite cover glass

panels.head: Satellites run on glass you've probably never heard of
panels.sub: Every solar array on a spacecraft is protected by thin radiation-resistant cover glass. Without it, the cells degrade fast in the harsh radiation environment of orbit.

panels.head: The U.S. can't make enough of it domestically
panels.sub: The Pentagon used Defense Production Act authority — the same lever pulled for steel and semiconductors — to fund expanded domestic supply, signalling the material is considered a strategic vulnerability.

panels.head: $7.1 M to Martin Materials Solutions
panels.sub: The small business is a specialist manufacturer of cover glass used on both solar arrays and thermal-control systems. The DPA award is designed to scale its production capacity, not fund R&D.

panels.head: Supply chain fragility reaches deep into the satellite stack
panels.sub: Constellation operators and defence primes face lead-time and sourcing risk on a component that sounds mundane but is flight-critical. A domestic capacity gap here can bottleneck entire launch manifests.

panels.head: Niche materials are the hidden chokepoint in the space build-out
panels.sub: For anyone designing spacecraft programmes or sourcing components: cover glass joins rad-hard electronics and specialty adhesives as items where single-source foreign supply is now a policy-level concern.

cards.title: Mermaid diagrams → ASCII art via WebAssembly

panels.head: Turn Mermaid diagrams into ASCII art — right in the browser
panels.sub: Two separate tools now render Mermaid flowcharts as plain-text ASCII, with no server required. Both run entirely client-side via WebAssembly.

panels.head: It started with a Grok-generated Rust library
panels.sub: Simon Willison first built a Mermaid-to-ASCII converter by compiling Rust code (originally produced by Grok Build) to WebAssembly. That worked — but wasn't the whole picture.

panels.head: A more capable Go library already existed
panels.sub: AlexanderGrooff/mermaid-ascii is a Go implementation with broader feature support, including colour output. Willison used Claude to compile it to WebAssembly too, making both tools runnable side-by-side for comparison.

panels.head: LLM-assisted WASM compilation as a rapid porting technique
panels.sub: Neither Rust nor Go library was browser-native. Claude handled the WASM build pipeline in both cases — the pattern: find a capable CLI tool, ask an LLM to wire up the WASM compilation, ship it as a zero-backend web utility.

panels.head: Any Go or Rust CLI tool is now a candidate for instant browser deployment
panels.sub: If you have a diagram, parsing, or data-processing tool written in either language, the friction to turn it into a shareable browser demo is now mostly prompting, not plumbing. Worth keeping in mind the next time you reach for a Python web server.

cards.title: India's first commercial rocket reaches orbit

panels.head: A private Indian rocket just made orbit
panels.sub: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 lifted off on July 18 and successfully reached orbit on its very first attempt — a result many first-generation launchers fail to achieve.

panels.head: First commercial orbital launch from India
panels.sub: Until now, orbital launch from India was the exclusive domain of ISRO. Vikram-1 is the first privately developed and operated Indian rocket to cross that threshold.

panels.head: Vikram-1 is a small solid-fuelled launcher
panels.sub: Skyroot has positioned Vikram-1 as a dedicated small-sat launcher. The article does not confirm payload mass or exact orbit parameters from this flight; those figures are worth tracking in Skyroot's follow-up releases.

panels.head: India's commercial launch sector just got a credible second actor
panels.sub: With ISRO's commercial arm (NewSpace India) and now Skyroot both offering orbital rides, international small-sat operators gain a third geography — alongside US and NZ/AU — for competitive launch pricing.

panels.head: Watch for payload pricing and cadence targets
panels.sub: A successful debut flight is a data point, not a business. The questions that matter next: What does Skyroot charge per kg? How many launches per year are they targeting? And can they hold that cadence against ISRO's own SSLV?
